observations: The `content` prop is trusted, arbitrary HTML (headings, links, lists, images) supplied by the implementer, so compliance depends on that markup providing meaningful text alternatives (e.g., alt text) for any non-text content it contains - the paragraph component itself renders whatever HTML it is given.
The component has no audio/video affordance of its own; upstream's compiled CSS supports embedded rich content generically but does not special-case media elements.

standard: 1.2.3 Audio Description or Media Alternative (Prerecorded) (Level A)

observations: The component renders `content` as a single block without imposing any structural constraint of its own (no heading-level enforcement, no required document outline); compliance depends on the implementer supplying HTML with correct heading levels, list semantics and label associations before passing it in.
The component renders `content` in the DOM order it is given, in a single block, so reading order in the shadow DOM matches the source order of the supplied HTML.
The component has no fixed orientation; its `size` variants and responsive heading/table rules apply purely by viewport width, not device orientation.
The component has no form inputs of its own.

Links within `content` are distinguished by underline as well as color (per the ported `a:not(.ct-button):not(.ct-link)` rule), so link-vs-text distinction does not rely on color alone. Compliance for any other color-conveyed meaning depends on the HTML the implementer supplies.

Text, heading, link, blockquote and table colors are all sourced from the `basic-content.light.*` / `basic-content.dark.*` / `content-link.*` / `table.*` design tokens, which resolve to CivicTheme's body/heading/interaction color primitives - the same token set audited for basic-content, chosen for AA contrast against the paired light/dark background.
All typography is set in `rem` units via the `--ct-typography-*` tokens, so text scales with the user's browser/OS font-size setting up to at least 200% without loss of content.
The component renders live text by default; compliance depends on the implementer not substituting an `` of rendered text for the `content` prop.

Unlike `ct-basic-content`, `ct-paragraph` has no centered max-width container or fixed-width wrapper of its own - it is `display: block` and inherits the width of its parent, so it reflows with the viewport without requiring 2-dimensional scrolling (the only fixed-width elements are the responsive `<table>` rules, which convert to a stacked layout below 768px).

The blockquote's stripe accent and table borders use the `basic-content.*.blockquote-stripe-background-color` and `table.*.border-color` tokens, sourced from the same theme-role palette audited for contrast against their adjacent backgrounds in basic-content.

Line-height, letter-spacing and margins are all token-driven `rem`/relative values with no fixed pixel line-height or `!important` overrides, so user style sheets can increase spacing without clipping or overlapping text.

standard: 1.4.13 Content on Hover or Focus (Level AA)
observations: The component has no hover/focus-triggered popover content of its own; link hover only changes text-decoration/color/background, it does not reveal additional content.

The only interactive elements the component can render are plain `<a>` links within `content`, which are natively focusable and keyboard-operable; the component itself adds no custom interaction or event handling.
The component has no focus-management logic (no modal, no state machine), so keyboard focus moves through it exactly as it would through the underlying HTML.

standard: 2.3.1 Three Flashes or Below Threshold (Level A)

observations: The component is a single content block, not a page region or set of repeated blocks, so bypass mechanisms are out of scope for it.
Any links within `content` receive focus in the same order they appear in the rendered markup - the component does not reorder content or use a non-zero `tabindex`.
Compliance depends on the implementer providing descriptive link text within `content`; the component does not alter or truncate link text.

If `content` includes headings, compliance depends on the implementer giving them clear, descriptive text and a level appropriate to the surrounding document outline - the component applies typography/spacing to whatever heading levels are present but does not choose or validate them.

Links within `content` get an explicit `:focus-visible` outline using the `outline.light`/`outline.dark` tokens (offset/width/color), matching the same focus-ring convention as `ct-basic-content` and `ct-button`.
The component has no sticky/fixed-position chrome of its own that could obscure a focused link within its content.

The component exposes no props that set an accessible name distinct from its visible content (no `aria-label`-style prop); any accessible names come entirely from the `content` HTML the implementer supplies.

Inline links within `content` inherit the surrounding text's line-height as their target size, which is standard for inline text links and generally exempted under the Level AA inline-text-link exception; compliance for any other interactive element embedded in `content` depends on that markup.
The component sets no `lang` attribute of its own; this is a page-level concern.
Compliance depends on the implementer setting a `lang` attribute within `content` on any passage that differs from the page's primary language - the component has no language-detection logic.
Focusing any link within `content` only ever triggers CSS hover/focus-visible styling; the component has no focus event handlers that change context.
The component has no form inputs of its own.
Every instance renders the same `ct-paragraph`/`ct-theme-*`/`ct-paragraph--*` class structure, so paragraphs serving the same purpose across the site are marked up identically.
The component is a content block, not a help mechanism.
The component has no form validation of its own.
The component has no form inputs of its own.

The wrapping `<div class="ct-paragraph">` carries no ARIA role of its own (native HTML from `content`, e.g. headings/links/lists, supplies its own implicit roles); compliance for any custom widget embedded in `content` depends on the implementer providing correct roles/states for it.
The component is static once rendered - it has no live-updating state of its own, so it does not need an `aria-live` region.
